rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and repository deposit context

next_steps: Seek a path-specific licensing or provenance statement from the CME maintainers.
Until then, retain unknown repository-artifact status and do not transfer the software-worded root license to upstream textual, edition, image, or encoding layers.
openQuestions.question: Does the pinned CME repository's root Unlicense expressly cover inherited U-M XML data, transcription, encoding, and markup at CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/ahb1378.xml?

next_steps: Ask U-M for the complete image inventory, bundle, and production record.
Compare any supplied images against both independently modeled Internet Archive carriers before asserting copy identity or derivation.
Clarify whether the current No Copyright–United States statement applies separately to OCR/XML and page-image delivery despite the generic 2006 warning.
openQuestions.question: Can U-M provide the complete historical page-image inventory or bundle, identify the physical copies scanned, and document the exact OCR-to-image production chain for AHB1378.0001.001?

next_steps: Seek a project statement identifying the U-M source version, conversion rules, and dates.
Ask for a path-specific license covering XML markup and metadata.
Preserve the verified main-reading-stream identity and apparatus-level differences without treating the two serializations as byte-identical.
openQuestions.question: What exact transformation pipeline, maintenance history, and license govern the Medictionary successor XML and its project-specific markup?

next_steps: Review post-2005 scholarship on the Robert self-reference, recension chronology, and manuscript stemma.
Seek the British Library's current codicological basis for the Cotton component date.
Retain separate attributed claims rather than forcing one normalized date or a certain one-author model.
openQuestions.question: Who composed and revised the two recensions, and how should the internally argued post-1297 or possible post-1307 longer-recension date be reconciled with Cotton Caligula A XI's current last-quarter-thirteenth-century catalog date?

next_steps: Search Wright's surviving papers and Trinity archival descriptions for Brock's transcript.
Ask Trinity and the British Library whether a transcript matching Wright's acknowledgment is identifiable.
Keep the transcript as a documented process layer with unknown survival rather than inventing a present holding.
openQuestions.question: Does E. Brock's working transcript of Cotton Caligula A XI survive, and where is it now held?

next_steps: Ask the British Library first about existing complete surrogates for Cotton Caligula A XI, Harley 201, and Add 19677 before commissioning photography.
Ask Bodleian for an existing or new complete Digby 205 surrogate; state explicitly that the current public object contains only f. 9r.
Ask CUL, Senate House, and Magdalene about existing complete legacy or preservation surrogates, current conservation limits, and complete delivered image inventories.

For any new order, request covers, boards and interiors, spine, pastedowns, all endleaves/flyleaves and blanks, stubs, every leaf recto/verso, edges if offered, and an explicit omissions list; negotiate repository redistribution and publication terms separately.
notes: No enquiry or reproduction order has been submitted; these are planned mediated routes, not pending external correspondence.
openQuestions.question: Can complete, reusable, whole-provider-object or whole-physical-codex facsimiles be obtained for the six witnesses without verified complete public delivery, and can Digby 205 be imaged beyond the published f. 9r canvas?

next_steps: For intended publication or redistribution, cite the exact manifest license and ask Trinity whether any additional permission is expected.
Do not weaken the exact item-level license by inference or ignore the broader institutional guidance.
openQuestions.question: How should Trinity's exact R.4.26 CC BY-NC 4.0 manifest license be applied alongside the Wren Digital Library's broader request for publication permission?

scope.notes: The successor preserves the aggregate 13,683 L, 197 LG, 61 DIV2, and 1,112 PB counts. The full BODY stream including apparatus is not identical: the successor changes the root vocabulary, element names and attributes, front-matter structure, availability text, milestone representation, boundary whitespace, and apparatus content such as a pinned GAP element rendered as literal ɯ.

reviewNotes: The lineage graph counts one intellectual work in the paired metadata manifest while preserving eight medieval witness identities, two Wright volume expressions, and distinct digital carriers.
Mostyn no. 259 and Senate House MS 278 are the same physical witness.

Each witness-to-recension version_of edge is explicitly scoped to the Chronicle textual component and used only as the schema-native proxy for carries or attests; it does not make the physical codex itself an abstract textual version.

The Medictionary successor preserves an identical note-excluded normalized BODY reading stream but transforms apparatus and structural markup, so it is neither collapsed into nor treated as textually unrelated to the U-M encoding.

lineage.summary: The pinned General Corpus XML copies U-M's 2006 uncorrected OCR of William Aldis Wright's 1887 two-volume edition. Wright principally follows Cotton Caligula A XI, uses a Brock working transcript as an aid, supplies a 152-line Cotton lacuna from Harley 201, and collates eight named sigla across longer- and shorter-recension evidence. Separate entities preserve the U-M historical page-image layer, a transformed current Medictionary successor encoding, the two Wright volume expressions, their two independent Internet Archive scan carriers, the physical witnesses, Wright's 1868 collation notebook, a complete provider-exposed Trinity facsimile, and a one-canvas Digby selection. Access and rights remain component-specific.

catalogSummary.author: Robert of Gloucester (attributed; identity and extent of responsibility uncertain)
catalogSummary.date: Both recensions after 1272; shorter recension after 1284; longer recension after 1297 and perhaps after 1307
catalogSummary.region: Middle English work associated linguistically with the Worcestershire–Gloucestershire area; exact place of composition unknown
catalogSummary.title: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester

catalogingSubject.label: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is one Middle English verse chronicle represented by William Aldis Wright's 1887 two-recension presentation. Wright's longer-recension main stream and forty-eight shorter-recension appendix blocks are representation and version boundaries within that one work, not forty-nine separate works. The unit excludes Wright's corrections and omitted front, glossary, index, and back matter as independent work content; it also keeps the medieval work, eight physical witnesses, Brock's working transcript, Wright's edition volumes, the U-M encoding, and the pinned repository artifact as distinct layers.

contentParts.label: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester
notes: The parent carries the single item and form count. Its structural verse metrics are zero because the two zero-item children partition all 197 LG and 13,683 raw L without duplication.
contentParts.summary: The single count-bearing intellectual work represented through Wright's longer- and shorter-recension editorial streams.

contentParts.label: Wright's longer-recension main stream

locators.value: Chronicle ff. 3r–13v and 15r–168v; two leaves missing after f. 74

notes: Zero item count prevents this recension presentation from duplicating the parent work count.
The raw L count includes the Harley supply and does not equal a canonical line range.
contentParts.summary: The main printed text, principally following Cotton Caligula A XI, with a 152-line lacuna after line 4920 supplied from Harley 201 and readings from the other collated witnesses.

contentParts.label: Wright's shorter-recension appendix presentation
notes: The forty-eight block labels are apparatus locators and version records, not forty-eight works or poems.
Zero item and item-form counts preserve the single-work boundary while the LG/L metrics report the raw represented verse.
contentParts.summary: Forty-eight editorial blocks A–XX containing shorter-recension additions and alternatives keyed to the main stream.

contentParts.label: Wright's corrections apparatus
notes: Editorial apparatus, not independent work content.
contentParts.summary: Two encoded correction divisions covering printed pp. 1010–1017, including results of Wright's direct rechecking of Harley readings.

contentParts.label: Omitted-matter placeholders in the U-M representation

locators.value: DIV1 types omitted front matter, omitted preface, omitted glossary and index, and omitted back matter; 223 placeholder PB

notes: These placeholders are representation boundaries, not additional work content or evidence that the omitted prose is part of the medieval Chronicle.
contentParts.summary: Structural placeholders for omitted front matter, preface, glossary and index, and back matter, preserving page sequence without encoding those source-edition sections.

assertion.notes: The lower bound is an internal historical argument, not a dated authorial document.

dateStatements.display: The shorter recension was composed or revised after 1284

scope.description: The shorter-recension tradition represented through Wright's appendix blocks and Greek-siglum witnesses

assertion.notes: The stronger post-1307 proposal remains qualified. It is not silently harmonized with the British Library's last-quarter-thirteenth-century date for Cotton Caligula A XI.

dateStatements.display: The longer recension was composed or revised after 1297 and perhaps after 1307

scope.description: The longer-recension main stream represented principally from Cotton Caligula A XI

assertion.notes: This is the first publication identified in the inspected edition history, not a claim that Wright's edition was the first print appearance.

dateStatements.display: First printed by Thomas Hearne at Oxford in 1724; Wright's source edition appeared in 1887
Legendary foundation of Britain through thirteenth-century English history; the shorter continuation reaches Edward I's accession in 1272

extent.description: One abstract verse Chronicle is counted once. The pinned XML has 61 representation DIV2 sections: 13 in Wright's main stream and 48 appendix blocks. It contains 197 LG and 13,683 raw L elements: 194 LG / 12,203 L in the main stream and 3 LG / 1,480 L in the appendix. The raw L total is an XML representation count, not canonical lineation: starred additions, a locally numbered 152-line Harley supply after line 4920, gaps, and merged printed positions prevent one-to-one equivalence with Wright's conventional line numbers. The 12,637 NOTE1 elements and corrections at printed pp. 1010–1017 are apparatus, not additional works or verse items.

formStatements.description: A Middle English historical chronicle in rhyming couplets. The pinned XML represents one verse work through 197 LG elements and 13,683 raw L elements across Wright's longer-recension main stream and shorter-recension appendix presentation; those raw element counts are not canonical lineation.

assertion.notes: The Chronicle compiles and reshapes earlier historical traditions rather than functioning as a neutral event record.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English and Latin occur in Wright's prefatory and corrective apparatus but are not counted as intrinsic languages of the medieval verse work.

Counting axis: one intellectual work; sixty-one XML representation sections; forty-eight appendix apparatus blocks; zero additional works from recensions, appendix blocks, corrections, or omitted placeholders.
The δ siglum is Senate House Library MS 278, formerly Lord Mostyn Library no. 259; the old and current shelfmarks are one physical witness, not two.
All physical, editorial, and digital source layers and their rights/access states are delegated to lineage:ahb1378.

next_steps: Review post-2005 scholarship on the Robert self-reference, recension chronology, and manuscript stemma.
Seek the British Library's current codicological basis for the Cotton component date.
Retain separate attributed claims rather than forcing one normalized date or one-author model.
openQuestions.question: Who composed and revised the two recensions, and how should the internally argued post-1297 or post-1307 long-recension date be reconciled with Cotton Caligula A XI's current last-quarter-thirteenth-century catalog date?

next_steps: Search Wright's surviving papers and Trinity College archival descriptions for Brock's transcript.
Ask Trinity and the British Library whether a transcript matching Wright's acknowledgement is identifiable.
Keep the transcript as a documented process layer with unknown survival rather than inventing a present holding.
openQuestions.question: Does E. Brock's working transcript of Cotton Caligula A XI survive, and where is it now held?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for item-level production metadata, source-copy provenance, and any preserved page-image package.
Distinguish the XML header's historical linked-image statement from currently verified downloadable image access.
Retain current No Copyright–United States treatment of historical materials without inferring an open markup license.
openQuestions.question: Which physical or scanned copy underlies U-M's OCR, what correction and page-image history applies, and what item-specific terms govern the XML markup?

assertion.notes: Fisher associates the original dialect with the Worcestershire/Gloucestershire area while resisting overprecise localization.
The conventional name is retained for discovery but not promoted to established sole authorship.

notes: The number and sequence of contributors or revisers behind the two recensions remain uncertain.

scope.description: Wright's 1887 two-volume edition and its two-recension presentation, not composition of the medieval work

assertion.notes: Wright names eight collated sigla. Add. 18631 and the Hunterian witness are expressly excluded from the collated set.

scope.description: Main text, variant notes, shorter-recension appendix blocks, and corrections

assertion.notes: The transcript was a material aid, not an exclusive textual route replacing Wright's direct consultation.

scope.description: Working transcript of Cotton Caligula A XI used in preparation of Wright's edition

subjects.label: Kingship, succession, conquest, and political legitimacy
Henry III, baronial conflict, and the Second Barons' War

summaries.value: A Middle English verse history that begins with the geography and praise of England and the legendary foundation of Britain by Brutus, then narrates British, Roman, Anglo-Saxon, Danish, Norman, Angevin, and thirteenth-century rule. Its later history treats the Norman Conquest, Stephen and Matilda, Henry III, Simon de Montfort, baronial conflict, and the transition to Edward I. The surviving work exists in materially different longer and shorter recensions whose authorship and revision sequence remain uncertain.

Wright prints Cotton Caligula A XI as the principal longer-recension main stream, supplies a 152-line Cotton lacuna after line 4920 from Harley 201, records readings from eight sigla, and places shorter-recension additions and alternatives in forty-eight appendix blocks A–XX. The U-M encoding retains the main stream, appendix, and corrections while representing front matter, preface, glossary/index, and back matter only as omitted placeholders.

The abstract Chronicle is counted once. Wright's main and appendix streams are zero-item representation children beneath that count-bearing work; the forty-eight appendix blocks are not poems or works in their own right. Corrections and omitted-material placeholders remain editorial/digital representation boundaries. Physical witnesses, working documents, edition volumes, scans, the U-M OCR, and the pinned XML are described separately in lineage.

assertion.notes: The wording qualifies the traditional attribution while retaining the established discovery title.

titles.value: The Chronicle attributed to Robert of Gloucester

assertion.notes: This is the source-edition title, not evidence that Robert's authorship is certain.

Its later history treats the Norman Conquest, Stephen and Matilda, Henry III, Simon de Montfort, baronial conflict, and the transition to Edward I.
